[Question]
How does the use of expression trees in Genetic Programming facilitate the search for optimal symbolic regression expressions, and why are reproduction and mutation operators designed the way they are in this context?

Expression trees represent symbolic regression expressions as tree structures where nodes are operators, functions, variables, or constants. This representation facilitates Genetic Programming (GP) by allowing solutions to be manipulated structurally. The reproduction operator combines good parts of two or more expression trees to create improved solutions, effectively recombining useful sub-expressions that correspond to subsets of the original problem. The mutation operator introduces small changes to expression trees to prevent the search from getting stuck in local optima, ensuring exploration of the solution space without drastically affecting fitness. Together, these operators leverage the tree structure to efficiently explore and optimize symbolic regression expressions.

3. Literature Review
Recently, many extensions to the cannonical GP or even new algorithms were proposed in
order to cope with the shortcomings pointed out in the previous section. This section will highlight
some of the recent publications that reported to have achieved improvements over previous works.
In [30] the authors propose the neat-GP, a GP based algorithm with mechanisms borrowed
from Neuroevolution of augmenting topologies [27] (NEAT) algorithm and the Flat Operator
Equalization bloat control method for GP. From the former it borrows the speciation mechanism
through ﬁtness sharing [24, 11], forcing the crossover operator to be applied only on similar solu-
tions and, from the latter, it maximizes the simplicity of generated expression by encouraging an
uniform distribution on the tree size among solutions of the population. The authors tested neat-
GP against the classic GP algorithm and the GP with the Flat Operator Equalization. Additionally,
the authors also tested diﬀerent variations of neat-GP by replacing operators, selection and ﬁtness
8
sharing mechanisms. For every tested function, at least one variation of the proposed algorithm
achieved the best solution.
Instead of modifying the search operators, in [17] the authors proposed the use of a surrogate
model to reduce the ﬁtness calculation cost, thus allowing a higher number of iterations within a
time frame. In their proposal, called Semantic Surrogate Genetic Programming (SSGP), the ﬁtness
approximation is composed of a linear equation controlling the number of training samples used
to calculate a partial ﬁtness from the expression tree and the application of the k-NN algorithm to
infer the remaining training samples. When comparing the results of SSGP against four other Ge-
netic Programming variants the authors showed that their algorithm could maintain a comparable
solution, and in some situations even better, than the contenders.
In [18] the authors also explored the use of a surrogate model to alleviate the cost of ﬁtness
evaluation. The proposed algorithm, Surrogate Genetic Programming (sGP), trains two Radial
Basis Functions Neural Networks [21] (RBFN) in order to ﬁrst map the semantic space (described
by the expression tree) into the output space (composed of the target vector) and then another
network maps the output space into the ﬁtness space. The sGP uses the surrogate model with 40%
of the population while the remaining solutions are fully evaluated. Comparing the sGP against
three other variants, the authors showed that their approach obtained the best results for every
tested function using the same number of function evaluations.

Given a set of terms T and a set of weights W associated with each term, the computational
complexity of evaluating the mathematical expression represented by T for a given d-dimensional
point x can be determined by means of the number of terms n and dimension d. Assuming a con-
stant cost for calculating any transformation function or the power of a number, each term should
evaluate d power functions plus a transformation function, each term should also be multiplied by
its corresponding weight. As such the computational complexity is O(n · (d + 2)), or O(n · d).
4.2. Symbolic Regression Search Tree
The general idea of the proposed algorithm is to perform a tree-based search where the root
node is simply a linear regression of the original variables and every child node is an expansion
of the parent expression. The search is performed by expanding the tree in a breadth-ﬁrst manner
where every children of a given parent is expanded before exploring the next level of the tree.
The algorithm starts with a set of terms each of which corresponding to one of the variables
without any interaction or transformation, as exempliﬁed in Eq. 5. This is labeled as the root node
of our tree and this node is given a score calculated by:
score(model) =
1

where MAE(.) returns the mean absolute error of the linear regression model learned from the
expression. After that, three diﬀerent operators are applied to this expression: interaction, inverse
interaction or transformation.
In the interaction operator, every combination of terms (ti, tj) is enumerated creating the term
tk = (Pi + Pj, id), the addition operation of polynomials is simply the sum of the vectors Pi and
Pj. Likewise, the inverse interaction creates the term tk = (Pi −Pk, id). Finally, the transformation
operator creates new terms by changing the associated function of every ti with every function of
a list of transformation functions.

[Question]
How does the symbolic regression search tree algorithm expand expressions at each node, and why is the breadth-first search strategy important for this process?

The symbolic regression search tree algorithm expands expressions at each node by applying three operators to the current expression: interaction, inverse interaction, and transformation. The interaction operator creates new terms by enumerating every combination of terms and adding their polynomial vectors. The inverse interaction operator creates new terms by subtracting polynomial vectors. The transformation operator creates new terms by changing the associated function of each term with functions from a list of transformation functions.

The breadth-first search strategy is important because it expands every child of a given parent node before exploring the next level of the tree, ensuring a systematic and complete exploration of all possible expansions at each level before moving deeper.

An important feature of our conjecturing program is the ability
to use theoretical knowledge.
If β is an invariant, proved to be an
upper bound for an invariant α, it can be a added to a theory list; the

We have been collecting independence number bounds for graphs for
some time: many are cataloged in [49]. The ten bounds recorded here
seem to be the most useful in practice. They should be interpreted for
connected graphs (although most hold for general graphs). These can
all be computed eﬃciently; thus the minimum of these upper bounds
and the maximum of the lower bounds are themselves eﬃciently com-
putable bounds.
Six Upper Bounds for the Independence Number of a Graph
(1) independence number <= annihilation number [43].

[Chunk 2]
the residue of the graph.
(3) independence number >= critical independence number [30].
The critical independence number is deﬁned to be the cardinality of
a certain independent set—and the theorem is trivial. It turn out that
this number equals the independence number for a large class of graph
(the K¨onig-Egervary graphs) which include the bipartite graphs.
(4) independence number >= max even minus even horizontal [23].
Let v be any vertex. It is easy to show that the number of vertices
at even distance from v minus the number of edges induced by these
vertices is a lower bound for the independence number. The max even
minus even horizontal invariant is the maximum of these values over
all of the vertices of the graph is then also a lower bound for the inde-
pendence number. Fajtlowicz deﬁned this invariant and observed that
it is very good in practice (at least for small graphs).
If the conjecturing program were given all published invariants
in a mathematical ﬁeld, all real-number operators used by mathemati-
cians, and all published bounds, the program would necessarily produce
new conjectures (not implied by existing theory) that are as simple as
any human can produce (with respect to the objects that it knows).
That is, if a human were to produce a simpler conjecture that is true for
all objects the computer knows then, necessarily and by the design of
the program, the conjecture must either be false for one of these objects
or it must be implied, with respect to these objects, by the conjectures
that the program does produce: that is, the produced conjectures must
give bounds that are at least as good as the human conjecture. The pro-
gram necessarily will consider every simpler conjecture: it iteratively
generates and evaluates every single syntactically possible statement
in order from the least complex to more complex. At the moment it
considers the human’s conjecture, if it does not produce the conjecture
itself, it is because it is either false, or not signiﬁcant in the described
sense.

(3) independence number >= critical independence number [30].
The critical independence number is deﬁned to be the cardinality of
a certain independent set—and the theorem is trivial. It turn out that
this number equals the independence number for a large class of graph
(the K¨onig-Egervary graphs) which include the bipartite graphs.
(4) independence number >= max even minus even horizontal [23].
Let v be any vertex. It is easy to show that the number of vertices
at even distance from v minus the number of edges induced by these
vertices is a lower bound for the independence number. The max even
minus even horizontal invariant is the maximum of these values over
all of the vertices of the graph is then also a lower bound for the inde-
pendence number. Fajtlowicz deﬁned this invariant and observed that
it is very good in practice (at least for small graphs).
If the conjecturing program were given all published invariants
in a mathematical ﬁeld, all real-number operators used by mathemati-
cians, and all published bounds, the program would necessarily produce
new conjectures (not implied by existing theory) that are as simple as
any human can produce (with respect to the objects that it knows).
That is, if a human were to produce a simpler conjecture that is true for
all objects the computer knows then, necessarily and by the design of
the program, the conjecture must either be false for one of these objects
or it must be implied, with respect to these objects, by the conjectures
that the program does produce: that is, the produced conjectures must
give bounds that are at least as good as the human conjecture. The pro-
gram necessarily will consider every simpler conjecture: it iteratively
generates and evaluates every single syntactically possible statement
in order from the least complex to more complex. At the moment it
considers the human’s conjecture, if it does not produce the conjecture
itself, it is because it is either false, or not signiﬁcant in the described
sense.

1. Introduction
Our Project began with the development of a program that can be
used to generate invariant-relation and property-relation conjectures
in many areas of mathematics. This program can produce conjectures
which are not implied by existing (published) theorems. Here we pro-
pose a new approach to push forward existing mathematical research
goals—using automated mathematical discovery software. We suggest
how to initiate and harness large-scale collaborative mathematics. We
envision mathematical research labs similar to what exist in other sci-
ences, new avenues for funding, new opportunities for training stu-
dents, and a more eﬃcient and eﬀective use of published mathematical
research.
The Graph Brain Project is an experiment in how the use of auto-
mated mathematical discovery software, databases, large collaboration,
and systematic investigation provide a model for how mathematical re-
search might proceed in the future. Our experiment is modular and
can be usefully expanded. We investigated one small open problem in
graph theory. In the course of this investigation we coded many graph

[Question]
How does the Graph Brain Project's use of automated mathematical discovery software, as introduced in the development of invariant-relation conjectures, facilitate the generation of new conjectured bounds for the independence number of a graph that are not implied by existing published bounds?

The Graph Brain Project employs automated mathematical discovery software to generate invariant-relation and property-relation conjectures across various mathematical areas. This software produces conjectures that are not implied by existing published theorems, enabling the exploration of new mathematical insights. By applying this approach to graph theory, specifically investigating the independence number of a graph—a fundamental and computationally complex concept—the project generated new conjectured bounds that go beyond all previously published bounds. Thus, the use of automated discovery software facilitates the creation of novel conjectures by systematically producing and testing relations that extend current mathematical knowledge, allowing the project to push forward research goals and uncover new bounds unattainable through existing theorems.

[Question]
How does the system's handling of multi-type requests, such as combining Sound of Music (SOM) tour recommendations and restaurant suggestions in Salzburg as discussed in Chunk A, explain the challenges faced by users seeking both a SOM tour like Bob’s Special Tours and a local dinner place such as St. Peter Stiftskulinarium on Christmas Day mentioned in Chunk B?

The system focuses on returning answers for only one x.type per query and does not attempt to disambiguate or link different x.attribute tags to their corresponding x.type values when multiple types are requested. This limitation causes challenges when users seek multi-type requests, such as combining Sound of Music (SOM) tour recommendations and restaurant suggestions in Salzburg. For example, while the system can recommend "Bob’s Special Tours" for SOM tours, it does not attempt to provide restaurant suggestions like "St. Peter Stiftskulinarium" for a local Christmas Day dinner. This constraint leads to partial answers and potential confusion, as the system cannot simultaneously fulfill both tour and dining requests in one response.

[Question]
How do different QA systems vary in their approach to representing questions, and what are the implications of these differences for handling multi-sentence versus single-sentence questions?

Different QA systems vary in their approach to representing questions by using distinct intermediate semantic representations. Many systems parse single-sentence questions into queries based on an underlying ontology or database schema, enabling structured understanding. Open QA systems use open-domain representations for factoid single-sentence questions. Recent neural models represent questions as continuous-valued vectors, capturing semantic information in a dense form. Some systems rely solely on information retrieval by selecting keywords from questions without constructing explicit semantic representations; these can handle multi-sentence questions but do not deeply understand them. The implication is that systems with explicit semantic parsing are more suited for single-sentence questions with structured queries, while keyword-based IR systems can manage multi-sentence questions but with limited comprehension.

pute a more meaningful joint metric over tagged
phrases. We deﬁne a matching-based metric that
ﬁrst matches each extracted segment with the clos-
est one in the gold set, and then computes segment
level precision using constituent tokens. Analo-
gously, recall is computed by matching each seg-
ment in gold set with the best one in extracted set.
As an example, for Figure 1, if the system ex-
tracts “convenient to the majority” and “local bud-
get” for x.attribute then our matching-metric will
compute precision as 0.75 (1.0 for “convenient to
the majority” and 0.5 for “local budget)” and recall
as 0.45 (1.0 for “budget”, 0.0 for “best” and 0.364
for “convenient to the majority ... like to see”).

semantic labeler under different conﬁgurations.
We ﬁnd that using a CRF based system using all
features gives us an aggregate F1 of 50.8. The
use of our CCM constraints have a signiﬁcant
impact on overall performance, raising aggregate
F-score by over 4 points.

the CoDL training procedure for semi-supervised
CCM boost the aggregate F-scores by a further
1.5 points. In order to see how useful our semi-
supervised approach is, we also train a CCM based
model that makes use of the complete crowd-
sourced dataset for training, by adding conﬂict-
ing labels for a question as two independent train-
ing data points. As can be seen, without a semi-
supervised approach, the noisy crowd-labeled se-
quences hurts the performance signiﬁcantly.
We also repeated the experiments using a BiDi
LSTM+CRF using only the pre-trained word-
embeddings as input features. We were surprised
to ﬁnd that it performs at par with the vanilla CRF
model that uses all our engineered features. This
speaks to the effectiveness of neural models even
in low data scenarios. Our further extension on
adding our features to the neural model yield a 5
pt performance boost. Both CCM constraints and
semi-supervision improve F-scores by one point
each.
Overall, our best model combines neural
features, hand-designed features, CCM constraints
with semi-supervised learning over partially anno-
tated crowd data.

[Question]
How do CCM constraints and semi-supervised learning together impact the performance of the semantic labeler, and why is using a semi-supervised approach important when incorporating crowd-sourced data according to the results presented?

CCM constraints significantly improve the semantic labeler's performance by raising the aggregate F-score by over 4 points. Semi-supervised learning with CoDL training further boosts the aggregate F-score by about 1.5 points. Using a semi-supervised approach is important because training a CCM-based model directly on noisy crowd-labeled data without semi-supervision hurts performance significantly. Thus, semi-supervised learning helps mitigate the negative impact of noisy crowd-sourced labels and improves overall accuracy.

How does the paper address the challenge of disagreement among crowd workers in labeling recommendation questions, and why is their approach preferable to discarding entire examples with any disagreement?

The paper addresses disagreement among crowd workers by only keeping the labels where two annotators agree and disregarding the other labels, resulting in partially labeled sequences. This approach is preferable to discarding entire examples with any disagreement because it preserves significant correctly labeled data instead of losing it entirely.

[Chunk 1]
Adversarial attacks
Within the machine learning community, an adversarial framework refers
to inputs deliberately designed to manipulate the expected behaviour of a
prediction model [9, 15]. The adversary usually picks data from distribu-
tions diﬀerent to those assumed when training the model, thus defeating its
prediction capabilities. The attacks usually consist of corrupted features or
distorted inputs. In our problem of interest, two adversarial attacks on the
GP toxic model have been recently suggested [6], which we formally charac-
terise as follows.
Obfuscation attack. In this attack the adversary modiﬁes the character
sequence of those words conveying most of the toxic content within the input
comment. The modiﬁcation takes advantage of the robustness of the human
visual system to recognise corrupted variants of text.
Such edits can be:
misspellings or symbol substitution, letter repetition, fake punctuation (in-
3
serting dots, commas or blanks within letters in the words). As a result, the
input text becomes disguised from its original character codiﬁcation. These
kind of obfuscations has been identiﬁed as homoglyph substitution and bo-
gus segmentation anomalies [14]. Using these stratagems, attacks have been
reported to eﬀectively reduce the toxicity score of an aggressive comment
down to a benign level [6].
Polarity attack. In this attack the adversary attempts to obtain high
toxicities for inoﬀensive comments that however exhibit profanity content.
This is achieved by simply negating toxic terms that eﬀectively swaps the
polarity of the comment to its opposite meaning or emotional intent. For
example, if the toxic word is an adjective, the polarity change is achieved
by inserting the particle “not” before the word. The modiﬁed comment still
contains the character sequence of the toxic term, misleading the model to
identify it as aggressive even though its deceiving neutrality may originate
from idiomatically uncommon or odd–looking sentences. This kind of vul-
nerability was also exploited eﬀectively in [6] to outwit the toxic model. We
remark that the adversarial intensity can be worsen by combining the two
attacks within the same input, i.e. negating obfuscated versions of toxicity
terms.
2.3

[Question]
How do the obfuscation and polarity adversarial attacks exploit different vulnerabilities in the GP toxic model, and what effect does combining these attacks have on the model's prediction capabilities?

The obfuscation attack exploits the GP toxic model's reliance on exact character sequences by modifying toxic words through misspellings, symbol substitutions, letter repetitions, or fake punctuation, disguising toxic content so the model reduces toxicity scores to benign levels. The polarity attack exploits the model's inability to detect negation by inserting negation particles like "not" before toxic terms, causing the model to misclassify inoffensive comments with profanity as highly toxic. Combining these attacks worsens adversarial intensity by negating obfuscated toxic terms, further misleading the model and degrading its prediction capabilities.

Google toxicity model
The Google Perspective (GP) model aims to score toxicity of harassment
comments in online platforms on a scale from 0 (“healthy”) to 1 (“very
toxic”) . The model was built based on large–scale datasets of abusive com-
ments, using crowd–sourced annotations to train machine learning classiﬁers
(logistic regression and neural networks, with bag–of–words features, see [17]
for details). Toxic content is deﬁned as “rude, disrespectful, or unreasonable
comment that is likely to make you leave a discussion” [1].
2.2

[Question]
How does the dataset preparation method use obfuscation techniques to create the obfuscation-50 and obfuscation-99 datasets, and why were these specific types of edits chosen with their respective probabilities?

The dataset preparation method created the obfuscation-50 and obfuscation-99 datasets by sampling a random value r ∼ U(0, 1); if r < p, the character was kept unchanged, otherwise one of three edits was applied: homoglyph substitution (60%), bogus segmentation (30%), or fake letter repetition (10%). These specific types of edits and their probabilities were chosen to generate obfuscated toxic comments, with homoglyph substitutions being the most frequent to mimic character similarity, bogus segmentation to alter word boundaries, and fake letter repetition to simulate typographical variations. This approach aimed to produce realistic obfuscations while preserving the original content's authenticity.

[Question]
How does the Google Perspective toxicity model's scoring mechanism influence the selection and generation of adversarial examples through obfuscation and polarity attacks in the dataset preparation process?

The Google Perspective (GP) toxicity model scores comments on a scale from 0 (“healthy”) to 1 (“very toxic”) based on the likelihood that a comment is rude, disrespectful, or unreasonable enough to make someone leave a discussion. In the dataset preparation process, comments with toxicity scores higher than 60% were selected to form a subset of toxic comments. This subset was then used to generate adversarial examples through two types of attacks: obfuscation and polarity attacks.

For obfuscation attacks, 1000 variations of each toxic comment were created by applying controlled character-level edits (homoglyph substitution, bogus segmentation, and letter repetition) to the toxic terms, with corruption rates of 50% and 99%. These edits aimed to obscure toxic words while preserving the original toxic intent, generating large datasets (obfuscation-50 and obfuscation-99) of obfuscated toxic comments.

For polarity attacks, the toxic comments were modified by adding negations (e.g., inserting "NOT") to invert the polarity of the toxic terms, thereby creating examples that challenge the model's ability to correctly interpret toxicity when polarity is altered.

Thus, the GP model's scoring mechanism directly influenced the selection of comments deemed toxic (above 60% score) and guided the generation of adversarial examples by targeting the toxic terms identified by the model for obfuscation or polarity inversion, enabling evaluation of the model's robustness against such adversarial manipulations.

[Question]
How does the aligned coded shuffling scheme reduce the communication rate in distributed learning, and why is minimizing the interference dimensions critical in achieving the improved bounds for R* worst-case?

The aligned coded shuffling scheme reduces the communication rate by forcing the interference seen by each worker to occupy the minimum possible dimensions. Minimizing the interference dimensions is critical because it allows for more efficient data delivery, thereby closing the gap between the lower and upper bounds on the worst-case communication rate R* worst-case. This interference alignment enables achieving the optimal or near-optimal rates, especially for storage values S = mN/K with m in {1, K−2, K−1}, improving the bounds for distributed learning systems.

[Question]
How does the symmetry in the summation over permutations contribute to simplifying the expression for the worst-case communication load bound in equation (35), and why is it important for determining the coefficients of |Dk,W| for different sizes of W?

The symmetry in the summation over all permutations of the ordered set (1, 2, 3, 4) allows the expression in equation (35) to be simplified by changing summation indexes, making the terms symmetric and equivalent. This symmetry enables rewriting the sum of entropies involving different permutations into a form where the coefficients of |Dk,W| can be analyzed uniformly. It is important for determining the coefficients of |Dk,W| for different sizes of W because it ensures that the coefficient calculation for any subset W of a given size is equivalent to calculating it for a representative subset (e.g., |D1,{2}| for |W|=1), thus reducing the complexity of finding these coefficients across all subsets.

Introduction
Owing to the parallelized nature of the distributed computing, and the abundance of computational
resources over a large cluster of workers, distributed computational frameworks can enable data-
intensive learning tasks and big data applications in a timely manner.
Distributed computing
comes at the unavoidable communication cost due to data transfer to the distributed machines,
and the data shuﬄing process among the distributed workers, which is a basic building block in
machine learning paradigms. The data shuﬄing block can arise in many applications such as: a)
random shuﬄing of the data-set across diﬀerent points before each learning epoch so that each
worker is assigned new training data, which is a common practice that provides statistical beneﬁts,
e.g., distributed gradient descent algorithm and its stochastic variations [1–3]; b) shuﬄing the
data-set across attributes to assign diﬀerent features (or attributes) to each worker, e.g., in mobile
cloud gaming systems [4]; and c) shuﬄing the data between the mappers and the reducers in the
MapReduce framework [5], where the reducers are interested in collecting the data with the assigned
“key(s)” from the mappers.
Another limiting byproduct of distributing the learning process over a large number of machines
is the latency caused by the stragglers, i.e., the workers slower than the average due to several factors
such as resource contention, disk failure, power limits, and heterogeneous processing capabilities
[6, 7]. The straggler problem usually limits the completion time by the slowest worker. Several
approaches to mitigate the stragglers eﬀect include a) scheduling redundant computations in [8–11],
such that any unexpected tardiness or failure of a worker can be compensated by another worker
doing the same computations; b) work stealing where the faster workers once they ﬁnish their tasks
take over the remaining computations from the slower workers [12]; and recently c) work exchange
based on the work conservation principle, where coarse heterogenity knowledge/estimation can be
used to reassign the work load according to the speed of the workers [13].
A promising research has recently emerged in large scale distributed computing addressing both
wired networks, where the computations are done over the cloud [14, 15], and wireless networks,
where the computations are done over small mobile machines removing the burden from the cloud
[16–18]. Distributed computing platforms can also be classiﬁed according to the underlying network
topology. In the master-worker setting, a centralized master node posses the whole data set and
assigns diﬀerent parts of the data to a set of distributed workers, which collaboratively learn a
shared prediction model to be averaged out at the master node later; while in the worker-to-worker
setting (also referred to as the MapReduce framework [5]), the distributed workers are mapped to
train diﬀerent parts of the data to calculate some functions, then the reducers collects the data
with the same “key” to compute each function separately.
The application of coding theory to overcome the communication and latency bottlenecks in

[Question]
How do the challenges of communication cost and straggler latency influence the design of distributed computing frameworks for data-intensive learning tasks as discussed in this section?

The challenges of communication cost and straggler latency significantly influence the design of distributed computing frameworks for data-intensive learning tasks by necessitating strategies to manage data transfer and mitigate slow workers. Communication cost arises from data transfer and data shuffling among distributed workers, which is essential for assigning new training data or features and for operations like MapReduce. To address straggler latency, caused by slower workers due to resource contention, failures, or heterogeneous capabilities, frameworks incorporate approaches such as scheduling redundant computations to compensate for slow or failed workers, work stealing where faster workers take over remaining tasks, and work exchange based on workload reassignment according to worker speeds. These challenges drive the development of coding theory applications and network topology considerations to optimize communication efficiency and reduce latency in distributed learning systems.

Main Contributions of this Paper
In this paper, we focus on the coded data shuﬄing for the wired master-worker setting, where
coding opportunities are created by exploiting the excess storage at the workers.
Before each
3
learning epoch, the data is shuﬄed at the master node for diﬀerent training data assignment at
each worker, which causes the communication overhead. On one extreme, when all the workers
have enough storage to store the whole data set, then no communication is needed for any random
shuﬄe. On the other hand, when the storage is just enough to store the assigned data, which we also
refer to as the no excess storage case, then the communication is expected to be maximal. Thus,
we aim to characterize the fundamental information-theoretic trade-oﬀbetween the communication
overhead due to shuﬄing and the available storage at the distributed workers. The contributions
of this paper are summarized next:
•
We ﬁrst derive an information theoretic lower bound on the worst-case communication overhead
for the data shuﬄing problem. We start by obtaining a family of lower bounds on the rate of some
chosen shuﬄes.
Since the rate of any shuﬄe is at most as large as the worst-case shuﬄe, the
obtained lower bounds serve as valid lower bounds for the worst-case rate as well. We then average
out all the lower bounds we get using the chosen shuﬄes. The key step here is choosing the shuﬄes
which lead to the best lower bound on the communication overhead as a function of the storage.
In particular, we consider a set of cyclic shuﬄes where no overlap between the assigned data batch
to any worker in the two subsequent shuﬄes. Based on a novel bounding methodology similar to
the recent results in the caching literature [38, 39], we are able to express the lower bound as a
linear program (LP). We then solve the LP to obtain the best lower bounds on the communication
overhead for diﬀerent regimes of storage.
•
Next, we introduce our achievable scheme based on a placement/update procedure that main-
tains the structure of the storage, which we refer to as “the structural invariant placement and
update”. The storage placement involves partitioning the data points across dimensions, which
allows each worker to store at least some parts of each data point. Through a careful novel stor-
age update, the structure of the storage can be maintained over time. This allows for applying a
data delivery mechanism similar to [40], which approaches the optimal worst-case communication-
storage trade-oﬀ(based on the obtained lower bound) within a vanishing gap ratio of
K
K−1 as the
number of distributed workers K increases.
•
Finally, we introduce new ideas on how to fully characterize the optimal worst-case communica-
tion overhead. We show that by considering more sophisticated interference alignment mechanisms,
we can force the interference seen by each worker to occupy the minimum possible dimensions. We

[Question]
How does the paper characterize the trade-off between communication overhead and storage at distributed workers, and what methodology is used to derive the lower bound on communication overhead?

The paper characterizes the trade-off between communication overhead due to data shuffling and the available storage at distributed workers by deriving an information-theoretic lower bound on the worst-case communication overhead. This is done by obtaining a family of lower bounds on the rate for some chosen shuffles, particularly considering a set of cyclic shuffles with no overlap between assigned data batches in two subsequent shuffles. The methodology involves expressing the lower bound as a linear program (LP) based on a novel bounding approach similar to recent results in caching literature. Solving this LP yields the best lower bounds on communication overhead for different storage regimes.

[Question]
How does minimizing the Holoentropy of each cluster after outlier removal contribute to the compactness of clusters, and how is this reflected in the objective function of COR?

Minimizing the Holoentropy of each cluster after outlier removal makes the clusters more compact because it reduces the uncertainty or disorder within each cluster, leading to tighter, more homogeneous groupings. This is reflected in the COR objective function as the summation of weighted Holoentropy of each cluster: 

min π ∑_(k=1)^K p_k HL(C_k),

where HL(·) is the Holoentropy of cluster C_k, p_k is the weight proportional to the cluster size, and π is the cluster indicator. By minimizing this weighted sum, COR ensures that each cluster becomes compact after removing outliers, rather than minimizing entropy over the entire dataset including outliers.

[Chunk 3]
Although tremendous efforts have been devoted in the cluster
analysis, most of the existing methods assume that all the data
points should be assigned a cluster label. In another word, there
are no anomaly data points for during clustering process. Unfor-
tunately, this is not always true, especially for the unsupervised
task. The potential anomalies or outliers inevitably degrade the
clustering performance. For example, few outliers easily destroy
the cluster structure derived from K-means and generate bizarre
distributions of Gaussian mixture model. To handle outliers or
noisy data, some robust clustering methods have been proposed
to recover the clean data. Metric learning aims to learn a robust
distance function to resist the outliers [5]; L1 norm is employed to
alleviate the negative impact of outliers on the cluster structure [6].
•
H. Liu is with Department of Computer Science, Brandeis University,
Waltham; Email: hongfuliu@brandeis.edu. J. Li (co-ﬁrst author) is with
Laboratory for Computational Physiology at Massachusetts Institute of

of Electrical & Computer Engineering, Northeastern University, Boston;
Email: {yuewu, yunfu}@ece.neu.edu.
Manuscript received XXX; revised XXX.
Beyond these, some methods aim to learn the more effective repre-
sentation with some constraints. Low-rank representation assumes
that the intrinsic or clean data lie in low-dimensional manifold [7];
subspace sparse clustering explores self-expression property with
sparse coefﬁcient for representation learning. Recently, consensus
clustering generates basic partition ﬁrst, and employs the basic
partitions as the representation for robust partition. Note that these
methods still assign the cluster labels for each data point, rather
than explicitly removing anomaly points.
To tackle the negative impacts of outliers during the clustering
processing, some unsupervised outlier detection methods have
been put forward from different aspects. Usually each data point
is calculated a score to identify the outlier degree, returning top
K outlier candidates. Local outlier factor is one of the popular
density-based methods, where outliers are identiﬁed by comparing
the local density of the data point and its neighbors [8]. Similarly,
local distance-based outlier detection uses the relative location of
an object to its neighbours to determine the degree to which the
object deviates from its neighbourhood [9]. Angle-based outlier
detection focuses on variance in the angles between the difference
vectors of a point to the other points, where the angles of the
outliers and other two randomly selected points have some devi-
ations [10], [11]. Other representative methods include ensemble-
based iForest [12], eigenvector-based OPCA [13], cluster-based
TONMF [14], and so on.
Although outlier detection methods can be regarded as a pre-
process for cluster analysis, outlier detection and cluster analysis
are usually conducted as two separated tasks. In fact, they are
strongly coupled. Cluster structure can be easily destroyed by few
outliers [15]; on the contrary, outliers are deﬁned by the concept of
cluster, which are recognized as the points belonging to none of the

[Question]
How do the Random Parameter Selection (RPS) and Random Feature Selection (RFS) strategies differ in generating basic partitions for COR, and why does RFS lead to improved outlier detection performance on certain datasets according to the results presented?

Random Parameter Selection (RPS) generates basic partitions by employing K-means clustering with different cluster numbers. Random Feature Selection (RFS), on the other hand, generates basic partitions by randomly selecting partial features for K-means clustering. RFS leads to improved outlier detection performance on certain datasets because it helps alleviate the negative impact of noisy features, producing higher quality basic partitions for COR. This improvement is demonstrated on shuttle and kddcup datasets, where RFS achieves better metrics than RPS, except for shuttle in terms of Rn.

[Context]
[Chunk 1]
Hongfu Liu, Member, IEEE, Jun Li, Member, IEEE, Yue Wu and Yun Fu, Fellow, IEEE
Abstract—Cluster analysis and outlier detection are strongly coupled tasks in data mining area. Cluster structure can be easily
destroyed by few outliers; on the contrary, outliers are deﬁned by the concept of cluster, which are recognized as the points belonging
to none of the clusters. Unfortunately, most existing studies do not notice the coupled relationship between these two task and handle
them separately. In light of this, we consider the joint cluster analysis and outlier detection problem, and propose the Clustering with
Outlier Removal (COR) algorithm. Generally speaking, the original space is transformed into the binary space via generating basic
partitions in order to deﬁne clusters. Then an objective function based Holoentropy is designed to enhance the compactness of each
cluster with a few outliers removed. With further analyses on the objective function, only partial of the problem can be handled by
K-means optimization. To provide an integrated solution, an auxiliary binary matrix is nontrivally introduced so that COR completely
and efﬁciently solves the challenging problem via a uniﬁed K-means-- with theoretical supports. Extensive experimental results on
numerous data sets in various domains demonstrate the effectiveness and efﬁciency of COR signiﬁcantly over state-of-the-art methods
in terms of cluster validity and outlier detection. Some key factors in COR are further analyzed for practical use. Finally, an application
on ﬂight trajectory is provided to demonstrate the effectiveness of COR in the real-world scenario.
Index Terms—Outlier detection, Clustering, Holoentropy, K-means--.
!
1
INTRODUCTION
Cluster analysis is a fundamental task in data mining and machine
learning area, which aims to separate a bunch of data points
into different groups so that similar points are assigned into the
same cluster. Although cluster analysis has been studied for long
time, it is still catching rising attention in industrial scenarios
due to its wide applications, from customer segmentation [1] to
information retrieval [2], and from recommendation systems [3] to
resource allocation [4]. Accordingly, cluster analysis has also been
extensively explored in the academia. K-means is one of the most
representative clustering methods, which seeks K prototypes as
the centroids to present the data points with the nearest distance.
Spectral clustering designed for graph partition, minimizes the
weights of cut edges to obtain disconnected sub-graphs with
roughly even sizes. Gaussian mixture model estimates K Gaussian
distribution with means and variances to ﬁt the data.
Although tremendous efforts have been devoted in the cluster
analysis, most of the existing methods assume that all the data
points should be assigned a cluster label. In another word, there
are no anomaly data points for during clustering process. Unfor-
tunately, this is not always true, especially for the unsupervised
task. The potential anomalies or outliers inevitably degrade the
clustering performance. For example, few outliers easily destroy
the cluster structure derived from K-means and generate bizarre
distributions of Gaussian mixture model. To handle outliers or
noisy data, some robust clustering methods have been proposed
to recover the clean data. Metric learning aims to learn a robust

[Question]
How does the Clustering with Outlier Removal (COR) algorithm integrate cluster analysis and outlier detection to improve clustering performance, and why is this integration necessary according to the authors?

The COR algorithm integrates cluster analysis and outlier detection by transforming the original space into a binary space through generating basic partitions to define clusters, then designing an objective function based on Holoentropy to enhance cluster compactness while removing a few outliers. It introduces an auxiliary binary matrix to enable a unified K-means-- optimization that efficiently solves the joint problem. This integration is necessary because outliers can easily destroy cluster structures, and outliers themselves are defined relative to clusters; handling these tasks separately ignores their coupled relationship, which degrades clustering performance.

[Question]
How do the different strategies for generating basic partitions, Random Parameter Selection (RPS) and Random Feature Selection (RFS), impact the performance of COR, and why might RFS offer improvements over RPS on certain datasets?

Random Parameter Selection (RPS) generates basic partitions by applying K-means clustering with different cluster numbers, while Random Feature Selection (RFS) generates basic partitions by randomly selecting partial features for K-means clustering. RFS can improve the performance of COR on certain datasets by alleviating the negative impact of noisy features, thereby producing higher quality basic partitions. For example, on the shuttle and kddcup datasets, RFS achieved better results than RPS in most metrics, with COR using RFS on kddcup outperforming RPS by over 5% in Jaccard and 7% in F-measure, and achieving competitive performance with the best rival while being significantly faster.

II. Ontology Summarization
As the size and the complexity of ontologies increase, there
is a high demand in order to facilitate ontology understanding
and help users take advantage of an ontology quickly. There
are ontology management [11] techniques that aim to reduce
the size and complexity of an ontology such as ontology
partitioning [12], ontology segmentation [13] , and ontology
modularization [14] but, they do not keep the most important
information. Diﬀerently, ontology summarization attempt to
provide eﬃcient and eﬀective models to grab knowledge from
an ontology while preserving informative nodes. The expected
outcome of ontology summarization, usually, is deﬁned as
sub-graphs or key nodes at the data and schema level that
represents the most important nodes (entities or concepts).

[Chunk 2]
16 Reference.
E. Identifying Key Concepts within an Ontology
Automatically identifying the key concepts within an ontol-
ogy applying topological and lexical criteria including Name
Simplicity, Density, Coverage, and Popularity is the main idea
behind [26] approach. The ultimate goal in Pires et al. [26]
model is to return a subset of concept from an ontology that
match as much as possible to those concepts produced by
human experts. This model is focusing more on returning key
concepts within an ontology without pay that much attention
to generating a graph of extracted important concepts.
F. Ontology Understanding without Tears
Troullinou et al. in [23] proposed an advanced version of
RDF Digest: Eﬃcient summarization of RDF/S KBs [33] as
a new automatically high quality RDF/S Knowledge Bases
summary producer. Finding the most representative concepts
within schema graph considering the corresponding instances
is the key point in generating the summary for an ontology. In
this context, the structure of the graph and semantics of KB are
playing important roles in the ﬁnal summary. In the proposed
approach, the importance of each node (concept) is determined
through the Relative Cardinality, in the next step the centrality
of each node in the KB is estimated by combining the Relative
Cardinality with the type and number of the incoming
and
outgoingedges in the schema. The ﬁnal step in this approach is
generating valid sub-schema graphs that cover more relevant
nodes by minimizing their overlaps. Two algorithms that try
to optimize the local and global importance of the selected
paths are applied in order to generate the ﬁnal summarized
sub-schema graphs.

[Question]
How does the approach proposed by Troullinou et al. in [23] for generating ontology summaries using Relative Cardinality and centrality metrics address the challenge of preserving informative nodes in ontology summarization as described in Section II?

The approach proposed by Troullinou et al. addresses the challenge of preserving informative nodes in ontology summarization by focusing on identifying the most representative concepts within the schema graph while considering their corresponding instances. It determines the importance of each node (concept) through Relative Cardinality, which reflects the significance of nodes based on instance data. Then, it estimates the centrality of each node by combining Relative Cardinality with the type and number of incoming and outgoing edges in the schema, capturing both semantic and structural importance. This dual consideration ensures that key informative nodes are identified effectively. Finally, the approach generates valid sub-schema graphs that cover more relevant nodes while minimizing overlaps, using algorithms that optimize local and global importance of selected paths. This process results in summarized subgraphs that preserve the most important and informative nodes, directly addressing the need in ontology summarization to retain key knowledge while reducing complexity.

[Question]
How do the approaches by Pires et al. and Troullinou et al. differ in their methods for identifying key concepts within an ontology, and what are the implications of these differences for the type of summary each produces?

Pires et al.'s approach focuses on automatically identifying key concepts within an ontology using topological and lexical criteria such as Name Simplicity, Density, Coverage, and Popularity, aiming to return a subset of concepts that closely match those identified by human experts, without emphasizing the generation of a graph of extracted concepts. Consequently, Pires et al. produce a subset of key concepts, whereas Troullinou et al. generate summarized sub-schema graphs that provide a structured and optimized representation of the ontology.

II. Ontology Summarization
As the size and the complexity of ontologies increase, there
is a high demand in order to facilitate ontology understanding
and help users take advantage of an ontology quickly. There
are ontology management [11] techniques that aim to reduce
the size and complexity of an ontology such as ontology
partitioning [12], ontology segmentation [13] , and ontology
modularization [14] but, they do not keep the most important
information. Diﬀerently, ontology summarization attempt to
provide eﬃcient and eﬀective models to grab knowledge from
an ontology while preserving informative nodes. The expected
outcome of ontology summarization, usually, is deﬁned as
sub-graphs or key nodes at the data and schema level that
represents the most important nodes (entities or concepts).

[Question]
How do the approaches by Pires et al. and Troullinou et al. differ in their methods for identifying and summarizing key concepts within an ontology, and what are the implications of these differences for the type of output each method produces?

Pires et al.'s approach focuses on automatically identifying key concepts within an ontology using topological and lexical criteria such as Name Simplicity, Density, Coverage, and Popularity, aiming to return a subset of concepts that closely match those identified by human experts. This method emphasizes selecting key concepts without generating a graph of these concepts.

In contrast, Troullinou et al.'s approach generates a high-quality summary of RDF/S knowledge bases by finding the most representative concepts within the schema graph, considering corresponding instances. It determines the importance of each node through Relative Cardinality and estimates node centrality by combining this with the type and number of incoming and outgoing edges. Their method produces valid sub-schema graphs that cover relevant nodes while minimizing overlaps, using algorithms to optimize local and global importance.

The implication is that Pires et al.'s method outputs a subset of key concepts without focusing on graph structure, while Troullinou et al.'s method produces summarized sub-schema graphs that represent the ontology's structure and semantics more comprehensively.

[Question]
Why does the paper use the CTC loss function in constructing audio adversarial examples despite it not guaranteeing minimal perturbations, and how does this choice affect the optimization process?

The paper uses the CTC loss function because it ensures that if the loss ℓ(x′, t) ≤ 0, then the transcription C(x′) equals the target t, making the solution adversarial. Although the converse is not guaranteed—meaning the perturbation may not be minimal—this property still produces effective adversarial examples. This choice affects the optimization process by allowing the solution to be adversarial but potentially with non-minimal perturbations, and it requires solving a modified optimization problem that balances perturbation size and loss, using iterative reduction of a distortion threshold to find a suitable solution.

Adversarial Examples. Evasion attacks have long been
studied on machine learning classiﬁers [4, 5, 29], and are
practical against many types of models [8].
When discussion neural networks, these evasion attacks
are referred to as adversarial examples [40]: for any input
x, it is possible to construct a sample x′ that is similar to x
(according to some metric) but so that C(x) ̸= C(x′) [8]. In
the audio domain, these untargeted adversarial example are
usually not interesting: causing a speech-to-text system to
transcribe “test sentence” as the misspelled “test sentense”
does little to help an adversary.
Targeted Adversarial Examples are a more powerful at-
tack: not only must the classiﬁcation of x and x′ differ,
but the network must assign a speciﬁc label (chosen by the
adversary) to the instance x′. The purpose of this paper is
to show that targeted adversarial examples are possible with
only slight distortion on speech-to-text systems.

[Question]
How do targeted adversarial examples differ from untargeted adversarial examples in their impact on speech-to-text systems, and why does the paper focus on demonstrating the feasibility of targeted attacks with only slight distortions?

Targeted adversarial examples differ from untargeted ones in that they force the speech-to-text system to assign a specific label chosen by the adversary, rather than just causing any incorrect transcription. The paper focuses on demonstrating the feasibility of targeted attacks with only slight distortions because targeted attacks are more powerful and meaningful, as untargeted attacks causing minor errors (e.g., misspellings) are usually not useful to an adversary.

4.1
Toy Problem
We start with a toy problem which is easy to visualize. Instead of generating weights for a neural
network, we generate a two dimensional vector. The goal is for the generated vectors have high
values on a speciﬁed Gaussian mixture, and also to obtain high diversity. We take the input z to the
hypernetwork to have dimension 1, to demonstrate the case where the hypernetworks output manifold
has a lower dimensionality than the weight space. This problem does not have symmetries, so we
do not include gauge ﬁxing in the loss. Instead, we use a conventional ℓ2 regularization loss. The
hypernetwork is taken as a MLP whose hidden layers have sizes 30, 10 and 10. The ﬁnal distribution
learned by the hypernetwork is displayed in Fig. 1. We see that the one dimensional distribution is
supported on a path which passes through all peaks of the Gaussian mixture. Inevitably, the path
must pass through regions with low values of the Gaussian mixture. However, in these regions the
density is lower.
4.2
MNIST
We take the target network architecture T to be a simple four layer convolutional network for
classifying images of the MNIST data set[23]. The full target network speciﬁcation is displayed in
Appendix A.6. The total number of weights in the network is 20018. This network can easily be
trained to achieve an accuracy of over 99% on the validation set.
For the hypernetwork, we take the input vector z to be 300 dimensional, drawn from a uniform
distribution. The extractor and weight generators have three layers each. The total number of
parameters is 633640. For a detailed speciﬁcation, including training details, see Appendix A.7.
We compare our results to MNFG[4]. We used code that is available online at https://github.
com/AMLab-Amsterdam/MNF_VBNN, and modiﬁed it so that it generates the same target networks as
our hypernetwork.

which yields lower accuracies, but they are more comparable to the results of MNFG and therefore
form a good basis for comparison.

diversity by showing that there is variance in the generated networks’ accuracies.

Introduction
In recent years, generative methods such as Variational Autoencoders(VAEs)[1] and Generative
Adversarial Networks(GANs)[2] have shown impressive results in generating samples from complex
and high-dimensional distributions. The training of these generative models is data driven, and
an essential requirement is the existence of a rich enough data set, which faithfully represents the
underlying probability distribution. A trained VAE decoder or GAN generator is a neural network
which is operated by feeding it with an input (usually random), and it outputs an array of numbers,
which represent, for example, pixel intensities of an image.
It is natural to suggest that this idea be extended from images to neural networks, such that a
trained generator would output numbers which represent weights for a neural network with a ﬁxed
target architecture and a ﬁxed task, such as classifying a speciﬁc type of data (By “weights” we
are referring to any trainable parameter of a neural network, including bias parameters). After all,
just like images, a neural network is a structured array of numbers. Indeed, this was carried out in
recent works[3, 4]. The term hypernetwork was coined[5, 3] for a neural network that acts as such a
generator. Applications include ensemble creation and Bayesian inference. Hypernetworks can also
serve as a tool for a researcher to explore the loss function surface.
A hypernetwork cannot be trained in a data driven manner in the same sense as the aforementioned
generative methods, since there is no rich data set of neural networks for each given target architecture
and each task. Thus, there is no underlying probability distribution of neural networks. We therefore
take a different approach, where we decide upon useful properties that such a distribution would have,
and express them as loss function terms. The two useful properties are accuracy and diversity. The
former implies that the generated neural networks achieve high accuracy in performing their intended
tasks. The diversity property means that the hypernetwork could generate a big number of essentially
different networks. Two networks are considered essentially different if their weights differ by more
than just trivial symmetry transformations.
Previous work [3, 4] are set up in a Bayesian context, require a probabilistic interpretation of the target
neural network’s output, and harness variational inference(VI)[6–8] to approximate the posterior of
the weights given the data and sample from it. This approach has some drawbacks, which our method
addresses. First, we do not require a probabilistic interpretation of the target network, which allows
us to apply our method to a broader range of neural network types. Second, our loss function has an
Preprint. Work in progress.

[Question]
Why does the paper argue that hypernetworks cannot be trained in a data-driven manner like VAEs or GANs, and how does it propose to overcome this challenge?

The paper argues that hypernetworks cannot be trained in a data-driven manner like VAEs or GANs because there is no rich data set of neural networks for each given target architecture and task, meaning there is no underlying probability distribution of neural networks. To overcome this challenge, the paper proposes deciding upon useful properties that such a distribution would have—specifically accuracy and diversity—and expressing these properties as loss function terms to guide the training of the hypernetwork.

[Context]

Abstract
Hypernetworks are neural networks that generate weights for another neural net-
work. We formulate the hypernetwork training objective as a compromise between
accuracy and diversity, where the diversity takes into account trivial symmetry
transformations of the target network. We explain how this simple formulation
generalizes variational inference. We use multi-layered perceptrons to form the
mapping from the low dimensional input random vector to the high dimensional
weight space, and demonstrate how to reduce the number of parameters in this map-
ping by parameter sharing. We perform experiments and show that the generated
weights are diverse and lie on a non-trivial manifold.
1

[Question]
How does the formulation of the hypernetwork training objective balance accuracy and diversity, and why is considering trivial symmetry transformations important in this context?

The hypernetwork training objective balances accuracy and diversity by formulating it as a compromise between the two. Considering trivial symmetry transformations of the target network is important because the diversity measure takes these transformations into account, ensuring that the diversity reflects meaningful variations in the generated weights rather than superficial differences caused by symmetries.

The main contributions of this work:
• We provide a simple template for hypernetwork loss functions, which have VI as a private
case. The loss function is applicable to any type of neural network task - not only to networks
with a probabilistic interpretation.
• We show how to make the measure of the diversity of the generated networks more mean-
ingful by taking symmetry transformations into account.
• We describe a parameter sharing architecture which reduces the size of the hypernetwork.
• We demonstrate for the ﬁrst time (to our knowledge) a hypernetwork that can generate all the
weights of a deep neural network, in such a way that all weights are statistically dependent.
We show that the set of generated networks lies on a highly non-trivial manifold in weights
space.

examples.

[Question]
How does the proposed hypernetwork loss function framework balance accuracy and diversity, and in what way does it generalize variational inference (VI) as a special case?

The proposed hypernetwork loss function framework balances accuracy and diversity by including an explicit hyperparameter that can be tuned to control this trade-off. It generalizes variational inference (VI) as a special case by allowing different forms for the diversity loss term, whereas VI specifically uses entropy. For certain choices of the hyperparameter and the diversity loss term, the framework reduces to VI.

5.3. Conclusions, limitations and future work
In this paper, we show a simple way to craft an adversarial
example that achieves an expected misclassiﬁcation rate in
the case of limited knowledge, in which the attacker knows
that the defender uses a logistic regression, but doesn’t know
the defender’s training data. We deﬁned an adversarial ex-
ample having an expected misclassiﬁcation rate of α by the
defender, as an α-adversarial example. Using 2 real-world
datasets, we show the importance to compute the intensity of
adversarial perturbations at the individual level: computing
an adversarial perturbation on the attacker surrogate model
and applying the same intensity across all perturbations is a
suboptimal strategy to achieve satisfactory intra-technique
transferability.
Our method can be used on any adversarial perturbation
technique that only uses the surrogate attacker model with-
out considering the defender’s model. But it is based on
the assumptions that (i) the attacker has a very large num-
ber of training examples, (ii) the defender has a very large
number of training data generated by the same DGP than
the defender’s data, and (iii) the speciﬁcations (optimization
method, regularization, hyperparameters, etc.) are known.
Moreover, to be computationally feasible, the number of
features p cannot be very large, because the computation of
the variance-covariance matrix of the parameters needs the
inversion of a p × p matrix.
Future research may be to:
• Extend our results to multinomial logistic regressions
• Add other penalization methods
• Use ﬁnite sample distributions to have a better estimate
of the variance-covariance matrix of the parameters
when the number of training data is not large
• Solve optimally the optimization problem 2 to com-
pute δ∗instead of the suboptimal solution λ∗
• Handle the additional constraints listed in section 1,
like x0 + δ ∈X
• Extend our method to other models that have known
asymptotic or ﬁnite-sample parameters distributions
• Evaluate the cross-technique transferability of α-
adversarial examples
• Extend the method of α-adversarial examples to the
case of unknown model, unknown model speciﬁcation
or unknown hyperparameters, but known distributions
of these elements.

[Question]
How does the paper justify the need to compute adversarial perturbation intensities at the individual level rather than applying a uniform intensity across all perturbations, and what implications does this have for achieving intra-technique transferability?

The paper justifies the need to compute adversarial perturbation intensities at the individual level because applying the same intensity across all perturbations, based on the attacker surrogate model, is a suboptimal strategy. Individual-level computation is important due to differences in scales of initial perturbations and variance-covariance matrix variations in different directions, which affect the effectiveness of the perturbations. This approach leads to more satisfactory intra-technique transferability of adversarial examples.

Arbitrary Intra-Technique Transferability of Adversarial Examples for Logistic Regression
The capability of the adversary may provide extra knowl-
edge on the defender’s model. The typical example is the
case of the attacker having feedback from the defender’s
model (Papernot et al., 2017).
The property of transferability of adversarial examples, de-
ﬁned by the fact that some adversarial examples designed to
fool a speciﬁc model also fool other models, was observed
by Goodfellow, Shlens, and Szegedy (2014), Papernot et al.
(2016a), Papernot et al. (2017), among others. Papernot et al.
(2016a) identify two types of transferability: intra-technique
transferability and cross-technique transferability.
The optimal L2-adversarial example for a logistic regres-
sion (with perfect knowledge) is the orthogonal projection
of the example onto the decision hyperplane (Moosavi-
Dezfooli et al., 2016). In part 4, we use this technique
to compute adversarial example, but our method can be
applied to any adversarial example crafting technique.
The intuition guiding our work our work is that an optimal
adversarial example for the attacker surrogate model, given
the limited knowledge of the attacker, may not achieve
satisfactory intra-technique transferability. If the adversarial
example is very close to the decision hyperplane, a very
small difference between ˆθd and ˆθa can lead to failed attacks.
In part 2, we provide a probabilistic deﬁnition of an adversar-
ial example. In part 3, we recall the asymptotic properties of
the logistic regression. In part 4, we develop a closed-form
approximate of an adversarial example having a chosen ex-
pected successful attack rate, in the threat model of known
model speciﬁcation but unknown training data for binary
classiﬁcation by a logistic regression. In part 5, we apply
our method on 2 datasets.
Contributions
• We introduce a new probability-based deﬁnition of
adversarial example having an arbitrary expected mis-
classiﬁcation rate using prior distributions to formalize
the attacker knowledge on the defender’s classiﬁer.
• We make use of the asymptotic distribution of lo-
gistic regression parameters to derive a closed form
method to craft adversarial examples having a chosen
expected success attack rate, in a limited knowledge
threat model. To our knowledge, this is the ﬁrst method
to allow an attacker to directly tune the probability of
attack success.
• We show that multiplying by the same scalar all adver-
sarial perturbations of the test samples computed on
the attacker’s surrogate model may not be effective to
improve intra-technique transferability.
• We observe the importance of knowing the estimation
method used by the defender, even for logistic regres-
sion.

[Question]
How does the asymptotic normality and variance-covariance structure of the logistic regression parameter estimators described in Chunk B facilitate the closed-form method to craft adversarial examples with a chosen expected success attack rate in the threat model presented in Chunk A?

The asymptotic normality and variance-covariance structure of logistic regression parameter estimators provide a probabilistic characterization of the uncertainty in the estimated model parameters. Specifically, the Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) ˆβMLE is asymptotically normally distributed with an asymptotic variance-covariance matrix given by the inverse Fisher information matrix (X⊺WX)−1, where W is a diagonal matrix of weights depending on the model probabilities. Similarly, the ridge estimator ˆβL2,λL2 is asymptotically normal with a known asymptotic variance-covariance matrix involving the penalty parameter λL2.

This asymptotic distribution allows modeling the defender’s logistic regression parameters as random variables with known mean and covariance, reflecting the attacker’s uncertainty about the defender’s model due to unknown training data. Using this probabilistic framework, the attacker can formalize the likelihood that a crafted adversarial example will cause misclassification by integrating over the distribution of possible defender parameters.

Consequently, the closed-form method to craft adversarial examples with a chosen expected success attack rate leverages the asymptotic normality and variance-covariance structure to compute perturbations that achieve a specified probability of successful attack. This is done by adjusting the adversarial perturbation based on the distribution of the defender’s parameters, enabling the attacker to tune the expected misclassification rate directly despite limited knowledge of the defender’s training data. Thus, the asymptotic properties of logistic regression parameters underpin the derivation of a closed-form solution for adversarial example crafting in the given threat model.

[Question]
How does the method proposed in this section address the challenge of achieving specific misclassification levels for logistic regression adversarial examples, and what role do perturbation intensities play in this process?

The method achieves specific misclassification levels for logistic regression adversarial examples by generating adversarial images that correspond to targeted misclassification rates such as 0.75, 0.90, and 0.95. Perturbation intensities, represented in absolute values and scaled, are used to adjust the adversarial examples to reach these desired misclassification levels, indicating that controlling perturbation intensity is key to controlling the level of misclassification.

[Question]
How does the choice of using the original label y0 instead of the model's prediction fd(x0; ˆθd) in the adversarial example optimization problem affect the determination of the perturbation δ0, and what implications does this have for scenarios where the original input is already misclassified?

Using the original label y0 instead of the model's prediction fd(x0; ˆθd) in the adversarial example optimization problem means that if the original input x0 is already misclassified by the model (i.e., fd(x0; ˆθd) ≠ y0), then the perturbation δ0 is zero (δ0 = 0p). This implies no modification is needed to cause misclassification. This choice affects limited knowledge settings by clearly indicating that no attack is necessary when the input is already misclassified.

[Question]
How does integrating a consistent personality into the core-chat module contribute to establishing emotional connections with users, and what mechanisms are used to maintain this personality throughout conversations?

Integrating a consistent personality into the core-chat module makes the chatbot easier to communicate with, more predictable, and trustable, which helps establish an emotional connection with users. The personality setting typically includes age, gender, language style, and areas of expertise. This personality information is encoded into a latent vector representation using deep neural networks, which influences response generation. Persona-based models are used to effectively integrate personality information in conversation generation, and models that control style and sentiment in language generation also contribute. The system continuously learns from user feedback and adapts to new circumstances to maintain the personality throughout conversations.

[Question]
How do the different categories of skills for social chatbots, based on target scenarios and properties, contribute to expanding the scope of their conversations?

The different categories of skills for social chatbots, based on target scenarios (personal or social) and properties (emotional or rational), contribute to expanding their conversational scope by enabling diverse interactions. Emotional skills in personal scenarios include self-management like mood simulation and episodic memory tracking, and proactive user comfort such as comforting users and expressing affection. Rational skills in personal scenarios cover engagement through personal event reminders, digital life assistance like weather and meeting updates, shopping assistance, and content generation such as composing poetry or drawing. In social scenarios, emotional skills involve role-playing, joke-making, babysitting, group activities like adding users and sending greeting cards, and emoji creation. Rational skills in social scenarios include image intelligence for recognizing objects, TV and radio hostess functions, and tools for device control and media recommendations. This variety allows chatbots to handle a wide range of conversational topics and user needs.

[Question]
How do social chatbots like Microsoft’s XiaoIce differ from traditional intelligent personal assistants in their goals and methods of interaction, and why is establishing an emotional connection important for their long-term engagement with users?

Social chatbots like Microsoft’s XiaoIce differ from traditional intelligent personal assistants (IPAs) in that their primary goal is not to solve all user questions but to act as virtual companions. They focus on establishing an emotional connection with users to better understand them and provide long-term support. Unlike IPAs, social chatbots interact through multiple modalities including text, speech, and vision, enabling more effective communication. Establishing an emotional connection is important for long-term engagement because it helps social chatbots understand users' needs over time and offer continuous support and assistance.

[Question]
How does XiaoIce transform an input image into a structured poem, and why are different components like keyword expansion, hierarchical RNN, and fluency checking necessary in this process?

XiaoIce transforms an input image into a structured poem by first recognizing objects and sentiments in the image to form an initial set of keywords. These keywords are then filtered and expanded by associated objects and feelings. Each keyword serves as a seed for generating a sentence in the poem. A hierarchical RNN is used to model the structure between words and between sentences, ensuring coherence and organization. A fluency checker controls the quality of the generated sentences. Keyword expansion enriches the content, the hierarchical RNN maintains structural coherence, and fluency checking ensures the poem's readability and quality.

[Question]
How do the values of ρ2 and ρ3 influence the quality of image reconstruction as measured by PSNR, and what does the variation in PSNR across different parameter settings suggest about the effectiveness of the analysis and synthesis operations?

Higher values of ρ2 and ρ3 generally lead to significantly improved image reconstruction quality as measured by PSNR. Specifically, increasing ρ2 and ρ3 from 10¹ to 10¹¹ raises PSNR from 16.09 dB to 288.01 dB, indicating much clearer reconstructions. The highest PSNR was achieved at ρ2 = ρ3 = 10¹¹ with ρ1 fixed at 0.1. This variation in PSNR across different parameter settings suggests that the analysis and synthesis operations are highly sensitive to these parameters, and optimal values of ρ2 and ρ3 greatly enhance the effectiveness of the frame-based image reconstruction process.

[Question]
How does the convexity of the set A(x) relate to the properties of dual frames, and what implications does this have for approximating solutions in sparse representation problems?

The set A(x) is convex because if φa and φb are dual frames of ψ, then any convex combination αφa + (1−α)φb (for α ∈ [0,1]) is also a dual frame. This convexity implies that if a solution u*₁ does not belong to A(x), its best approximation u*₂ within A(x) can be found, ensuring that approximations to solutions in sparse representation problems can be effectively obtained within this convex set.

Conclusion
We have presented a new formulation of text correction problem, where the
goal is to find an inaccuracy in a video description, and fix it by replacing
the inaccurate word. We propose a novel approach to leverage both textual
and visual features to detect and fix the inaccurate sentences, and we show the
superior results are obtained our approach. Moreover, we introduce an approach
to generate a suitable dataset for VTC problem. Our proposed method provides
a strong baseline for inaccuracy detection and correction tasks for sentences with
one or multiple inaccuracies. We believe that our work is a step forward in the
research related to intersection of Natural Language Processing and Computer
Vision. We hope that this work lead to more exciting future researches in VTC.
